title: The Cullet Ceiling
passage:
For years the pitch to any glassmaker has run the same way: the more recycled glass you tip into the furnace, the greener the bottle that comes out the other end. Crushed waste glass — cullet — melts at a lower temperature than the raw mixture of sand, soda ash, and limestone it stands in for, so each load of it that goes in ought to trim the fuel the furnace burns and the carbon it breathes out. Fill the batch with cullet, the argument runs, and you save energy and landfill in a single stroke. It is a clean story, and it has shaped collection targets across the industry. What the story mostly skips is the awkward question of what happens when the recycled glass arriving at the furnace door is not clean at all.

That question was finally put to a working furnace. At the Marholm container works, a team led by the glass chemist Ottilie Brandt ran a season of matched melting campaigns, holding everything steady but the cullet — its share of the batch, and where it came from. Some campaigns were fed cullet that had been sorted by color and picked clean of everything but glass; others were fed the mixed stream that arrives straight from municipal collection, caps and crockery and all. Meters on the fuel line logged what each campaign burned. On energy the result was just as advertised, and pleasingly straight: each added tenth of cullet cut the furnace's fuel use by roughly three percent, clean stream or dirty, right across the range they tested. On the fuel line alone, more cullet was simply better.

Then Brandt's team looked past the fuel line at the glass itself, and the two streams parted company. Municipal cullet carries a small, stubborn load of things that are not glass — a chip of ceramic, a shard of stoneware, a fleck of stone off the roadside — and these do not melt at the temperature glass does. They ride on through the furnace as "stones", hard little inclusions that seed cracks and force the flawed glass to be pulled and melted a second time. With clean, sorted cullet the reject rate barely stirred, even when cullet ran most of the batch. With the mixed municipal stream it edged up slowly at first and then, once cullet passed about sixty percent, climbed sharply: each further ton of dirty glass carried its fixed freight of stones, and remelting the rejects handed back much of the fuel the cullet had saved. Read only the fuel line, and the greenest furnace is the fullest one. Read the reject bin too, and it is not.

This is not how the case is usually made. Rasmus Feld, who runs the regional glass-collection program that feeds the works, makes it with no such hedging. "Fill every furnace to the top with cullet — always, on every batch," he told me. "There is no such thing as too much recycled glass. If the melt comes out flawed, you clean the stream; you do not cut the cullet." Brandt is more guarded. The stream can indeed be cleaned, she agrees, but only so far and never for free: the last chips of ceramic are the hardest and dearest to pick out, and no municipal system she knows of delivers glass clean enough to run at the very top of the range without the stones coming in behind it.

The season's record, read whole, is more divided than Feld's rule allows. It does not show that cullet is a false economy — the fuel savings are real, and on a clean, sorted stream they run all the way up. What it shows is that "more cullet" and "greener glass" hold together only while the glass going in stays clean; past that point, on the mixed stream most works actually receive, the stones win back what the furnace saved. The number that matters, in the end, is not how full the batch is. It is how clean. A furnace fed the best cullet can swallow almost any amount of it. A furnace fed the worst has a ceiling, and it sits lower than the collection targets like to admit.
— Cornelia Stahl

cullet = crushed waste glass added to the raw furnace batch
stones = hard, unmelted inclusions left in glass by non-glass contaminants

questions.prompt: What is this text mainly about?

options.text: That crushed recycled glass melts at a lower temperature than raw batch and so cuts the fuel a furnace has to burn.
That municipal recycling schemes ought to sort their glass by color before any of it is sent on to a furnace.
That recycled glass saves real furnace energy, but only stays the greener choice while the incoming glass is clean enough.
That contamination in recycled glass can force flawed containers to be pulled from the line and melted a second time.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-004 (main idea / whole-text gist), edge position 1. WHY-KEY (C): the arc runs phenomenon (cullet pitched as straightforwardly greener) -> evidence (the matched furnace campaigns, ~3% fuel cut per added tenth of cullet) -> complication (the mixed municipal stream's reject rate climbs sharply past about sixty percent, and 'remelting the rejects handed back much of the fuel the cullet had saved') -> verdict, paragraph 5: '"more cullet" and "greener glass" hold together only while the glass going in stays clean ... The number that matters, in the end, is not how full the batch is. It is how clean.' C paraphrases that bounded verdict without lifting any distinctive four-word run. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = scope_error -- promotes the opening mechanism ('Crushed waste glass ... melts at a lower temperature ... so each load ... ought to trim the fuel') to the thesis; true and vivid, but it is the pitch the passage sets out to qualify, not its point. B = surface_word_match / outside_knowledge -- recycles the 'sorted by color' vocabulary into a policy recommendation the text never issues; the passage discusses the cost of cleaning the stream but never tells municipal schemes what they ought to do. D = detail-as-main / wrong_location -- accurately reports the paragraph-3 remelt mechanism ('force the flawed glass to be pulled and melted a second time') but that is a sub-point inside the complication, never the whole-text focus; tempting because it is a concrete, memorable detail.

questions.prompt: What does the text say about the furnace's fuel use across the melting campaigns?

options.text: Each added tenth of cullet cut fuel use by about three percent, whether the stream was clean or dirty.
Each added tenth of cullet cut fuel use by a fixed three percent, and the savings went on growing without any limit.
Fuel use dropped only on the campaigns fed clean, color-sorted cullet, and not on the mixed municipal ones.
The fuel savings came chiefly from the lower carbon locked inside the recycled glass compared with raw batch.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-001 (direct detail retrieval), local order. WHY-KEY (A): paragraph 2 states 'each added tenth of cullet cut the furnace's fuel use by roughly three percent, clean stream or dirty, right across the range they tested.' A paraphrases both the size ('about three percent') and the stream-independence ('whether the stream was clean or dirty'). WHY-DISTRACTORS: B = quantifier_upgrade (the engineered dominant trap) -- keeps the real figure but absolutizes the scope, turning 'roughly three percent ... right across the range they tested' into a 'fixed' rate 'growing without any limit'; the passage claims the saving only within the tested range, not beyond it. C = wrong_location / cross-metric swap -- the clean-versus-dirty split belongs to the reject rate (paragraph 3), not the fuel line, which fell the same 'clean stream or dirty'; tempting because the clean/dirty contrast is the passage's main distinction. D = outside_knowledge -- supplies a plausible-sounding mechanism ('lower carbon locked inside the glass') the passage never states; the text attributes the saving to cullet melting at a lower temperature, not to its carbon content.

questions.prompt: According to the text, what happened to the reject rate when the furnace was fed the mixed municipal cullet?

options.text: It stayed almost flat throughout, barely stirring even when the cullet ran most of the batch.
It jumped to its highest level the moment any municipal cullet at all was added to the batch.
It fell steadily as the operators learned to screen the caps and crockery out ahead of melting.
It rose only slowly at low fractions but climbed sharply once cullet passed about sixty percent of the batch.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-001 (direct detail retrieval), local order. STEM-LAW: the stem names the setup (the mixed municipal stream) but does not entail the direction -- the reject rate could in principle have stayed flat or spiked at once, so the answer is a reported empirical shape. WHY-KEY (D): paragraph 3 states that with the mixed municipal stream the reject rate 'edged up slowly at first and then, once cullet passed about sixty percent, climbed sharply.' D paraphrases that two-stage curve and its sixty-percent hinge. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = wrong_location / cross-stream swap -- 'stayed almost flat ... even when cullet ran most of the batch' is what the passage reports for the CLEAN sorted stream ('the reject rate barely stirred'), not the mixed one the stem asks about; tempting because the sentence is genuinely in the text. B = quantifier_upgrade / time-shift -- overshoots 'edged up slowly at first' into an immediate jump 'the moment any municipal cullet at all was added'; tempting to a reader who remembers only that the dirty stream went bad. C = outside_knowledge -- a plausible operational improvement (screening contaminants out) the passage never reports happening; tempting because caps and crockery are named as the contaminants.

questions.prompt: What does the text imply about why clean cullet can make up most of the batch while municipal cullet cannot?

options.text: Because clean cullet, being purer, melts at a lower temperature than municipal cullet and so saves more fuel per ton.
Because the contamination behind the defects comes in with the dirty glass, so more of it means more stones.
Because municipal glass can never be cleaned well enough to melt without flaws under any circumstances whatever.
Because the clean cullet had been sorted by color beforehand while the municipal stream had not been sorted.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-002 (inference / implication), one logical inch beyond the text. WHY-KEY (B): paragraph 3 reports that municipal cullet 'carries a small, stubborn load of things that are not glass ... and these do not melt', becoming 'stones', and that 'each further ton of dirty glass carried its fixed freight of stones', while clean cullet's reject rate 'barely stirred, even when cullet ran most of the batch.' The one-inch inference is that the defects scale with the dirty glass because the contamination comes in bound to it -- B states exactly this without overstating. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = wrong mechanism / contradicted -- the passage says the fuel saving was the same 'clean stream or dirty', so clean cullet does NOT save more fuel per ton; tempting because it sounds like a natural advantage of cleaner glass. C = quantifier_upgrade / too_far -- inflates a bounded difficulty into the absolute 'never ... under any circumstances', which contradicts Brandt's own view that the stream 'can indeed be cleaned ... but only so far'. D = too_literal -- restates the surface detail that the clean cullet was 'sorted by color', but color sorting is not what removes the ceramic and stone that make the stones; the literal detail does not explain the reject mechanism the question asks about.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's attitude toward Feld's claim that a furnace can never hold too much cullet?

options.text: Broadly persuaded: she largely accepts Feld's rule and treats the contamination as a minor snag that better sorting will clear.
Qualified: she accepts that cullet saves real energy but holds that maxing it out backfires once the incoming stream is dirty.
Convinced: she shares Feld's certainty that there is no such thing as feeding a furnace too much recycled glass.
Dismissive: she concludes that the fuel a furnace saves on cullet is too slight to be worth any of the bother.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-005 (stance / tone / attitude), edge position 5; signature trap is attribution swap. Hedge-balanced by design: options A and B are BOTH cautious/qualified in form, so the item cannot be solved by the 'pick the only hedged option among absolutes' structural tell (a documented G-STEM kill) -- the discriminator is direction and content, not hedge form. WHY-KEY (B): Feld states the absolute rule ('Fill every furnace to the top with cullet -- always, on every batch. There is no such thing as too much recycled glass'), but the writer answers in paragraph 5 that 'The season's record, read whole, is more divided than Feld's rule allows', granting that 'the fuel savings are real' while insisting that 'more cullet' and 'greener glass' hold together 'only while the glass going in stays clean' and that a furnace fed the worst stream 'has a ceiling'. 'Qualified' names exactly this real-but-bounded position -- energy gain accepted, the absolute rejected. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = direction reversal, hedged in form (the trap) -- it is just as cautious-sounding as the key ('largely', 'broadly'), but it lands the writer on Feld's side and downgrades the contamination to 'a minor snag that better sorting will clear'; the passage does the opposite, treating the stones as the binding ceiling and noting that 'no municipal system she knows of delivers glass clean enough to run at the very top of the range'. C = role_or_attribution_swap (the signature TYPE-005 trap) -- assigns Feld's quoted certainty to the writer; tempting because his 'always, on every batch' line is the most emphatic stance in the passage. D = polarity overshoot -- pushes the writer's reservation into outright dismissal, which the concession that 'the fuel savings are real, and on a clean, sorted stream they run all the way up' plainly contradicts.

fictional_entities: Ottilie Brandt, glass chemist and study lead (invented; all findings invented)
Rasmus Feld, director of a regional glass-collection program (invented; all quotes invented)
generator_meta.question_geometry: Q1 main idea (edge, pos1) / Q2 detail (fuel) / Q3 detail-comparison (STEM-LAW: stem names the mixed stream, direction is a reported outcome) / Q4 inference (contamination scaling mechanism) / Q5 stance (edge, pos5, HEDGE-BALANCED)
generator_meta.key_letters: C, A, D, B, B -- all four letters used, no monotonic run and no positional/alternation tell; keys mix confident-specific (Q1 thesis, Q3 two-stage curve, Q4 mechanism) with bounded/qualified (Q2 'about', Q5 'qualified') so 'pick the hedged answer' does not score the unit blind
generator_meta.engineered_traps: quantifier_upgrade at Q2/B ('right across the range tested' -> 'growing without any limit') and Q4/C ('cleaned only so far' -> 'never ... under any circumstances'); cross-stream/cross-metric swap at Q2/C and Q3/A (clean-stream flat reject curve offered for the mixed stream); attribution_swap at Q5/C (Feld's absolute assigned to the writer)
generator_meta.stance_item_fix: Q5 is the regenerated batch-3 kill slot. The batch-3 failure was a stance item whose key was the SOLE hedged option among absolute distractors (a G-STEM structural-leak kill: 'the only hedged/qualified option among absolutes'). Fixed here by planting a SECOND hedged option (A, 'Broadly persuaded') that is equally cautious in form but reverses the writer's direction (sides with Feld, calls contamination a minor fixable snag). Key and A are both qualified; the item is decided by content/direction only, so the form-tell is gone and the key is not the lone hedge.
generator_meta.schema_note: Emits 5 questions per the long_passage_5q blueprint (ELF long passages are invariantly 5q); this intentionally exceeds candidate-item.schema.json questions.maxItems=4, which was authored for single-question seeded eval items. All other schema constraints pass as-is.
generator_meta.self_blind_solve: Solved all five from the passage alone before consulting keys: Q1=C, Q2=A, Q3=D, Q4=B, Q5=B (5/5 match). No item blind-solved to two defensible answers; the Q5 two-hedge design was verified to require reading the verdict paragraph to separate B from A.
